stem: A manufacturing study measures defect rate across 5 factorys, each containing multiple production lines. factory A has 8 observations with mean 36.4 % (within-factory SD = 6.8). factory B has 8 observations with mean 46.6 % (within-factory SD = 4.1). factory C has 5 observations with mean 73.0 % (within-factory SD = 7.6). factory D has 10 observations with mean 35.3 % (within-factory SD = 5.0). factory E has 20 observations with mean 53.0 % (within-factory SD = 4.9). The between-factory standard deviation is uncertain, estimated between 6.7 and 16.8 %. Using a hierarchical Bayesian model, estimate the true defect rate for factory B, accounting for shrinkage toward the grand mean and the uncertainty in the between-factory variation.

ground_truth.answer: Hierarchical estimate for factory B: 46.6 % (shrunk from raw 46.6). Range [46.6, 46.7] as between-factory SD varies from 6.7 to 16.8. Pooled mean: 47.9.

solution_steps: Compute pooled (grand) mean: 47.9 % (weighted average of 5 factory means).
Shrinkage factor for factory B: B = σ²_between / (σ²_between + σ²_within/8). At σ_between=11.2: B = 0.984.
Interval [46.6, 46.7] captures uncertainty from the unknown between-group variance.
common_failure_modes: Using the raw observed mean (46.6) without shrinkage
Using only the grand mean (47.9) and ignoring group-specific data
Not recognizing that small-sample groups should be shrunk more toward the grand mean
Treating the between-group variance as known rather than uncertain
